data.title: Asset Entities: 'Yes' Vote For The Win - Strong Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The next few weeks are critical for Asset Entities (ASST) stock. The SEC, back in August, declared the upcoming merger with Strive allowed. Now, everything depends on a special shareholder meeting that will take place on September 9th to decide if the merger goes through or not. Here’s the catch: about 40% of the vote has already been secured in favor of the merger via the company board. With a majority needed to rule, they require just an additional 11% from shareholders for the merger to proceed. If Asset Entities were to merge with Strive, shares would continue trading under the same ticker and should undergo a significant revaluation upwards. Hence, I'm maintaining my strong buy rating on the stock as I see odds lined up in favor of this going through.

In my previous coverage of Asset Entities, I initiated with a strong buy rating, and while we saw the stock go through large upward swings, I will admit I published my buy call a little too early. The stock is up 68% on the one-month chart but is down around 6% since my strong buy. With the merger likely to go through, I feel the market can now properly value this company as a Bitcoin treasury moving forward, and I see the stock price reacting positively. The good news is that the company still holds over $750 million via the previous PIPE investment, ready to open up a position in Bitcoin at the right time. Other companies, such as Sharplink Gaming (SBET), have gone through similar transition periods, which saw their stock trading at much higher premiums after the move to a crypto treasury. Asset Entities is still behind in terms of their timeline for the transition; they’re yet to own any crypto. However, after next week, we could see them take a very big step towards becoming a treasury.

Financials

For the most recent quarter, the company brought in just $173,000 in 2Q25, a figure that is up 86% year over year. For the first half of 2025, revenue was $344,000, up 58% from $218,000 in the same period last year. EPS was around a $0.17 loss, much improved from the $0.58 loss last year. If we look at these numbers alone, growth is improving, yes, but their revenue is still tiny compared to total costs. Operating expenses came in at $2.87 million vs. the $1.82 million spent in the same period last year. G&A expense was about $936,000, up 24% year over year, and management comp was $1.8 million, up 90% since 2Q24. Contract labour stayed steady at roughly $132,000. In terms of cash, the company still holds $2.52 million, with total assets at $3.26 million. The bottom line here is that while still tiny, Asset Entities is growing. We’re seeing a heavy spend on management comp and G&A continue, which is driving those losses, but equity raisings are plugging that gap; stock issuance contributed $3.1 million in 1H25 alone. They hold a very thin cash cushion for their kind of business at just 2.5 million. I don’t see this lasting long without new financing.

An Upcoming Merger

The most recent news out of Asset Entities is that the merger with Strive Enterprises was declared effective by the SEC on August 22nd of last month. The combined company will have a name change, now called Strive, Inc., but it will continue trading under the ticker ASST. Any Strive shareholders, including equity award holders, will own about 94.2% of the merged entity. It leaves Asset Entities holding about 5.8%. Matt Cole, the CEO of Strive, will become chairman of the combined company, with Arshia Sarkhani, the Asset Entities CEO, becoming CMO and board member.

Asset Entities also filed an S-4 registration statement with the SEC. It’s a registration statement that companies must file with the SEC when issuing new shares in connection with a merger or acquisition. The SEC declared this effective on the 22nd, which means regulators have finished their review, and Asset Entities are now legally allowed to move forward with soliciting shareholder votes. The company will be able to issue new shares if the merger is approved. Keep in mind that this does not mean the SEC endorses the deal; it simply means the disclosure is sufficient and investors have the full information needed to vote. That brings us to the upcoming shareholder vote next week on September 9th. Because the merger involves issuing new shares to Strive’s shareholders, Asset Entities’ shareholders must, of course, first approve. The company has set a record date of July 21st of this year, so only investors who owned shares as of that date can vote. The Asset Entities board has already lined up roughly 40% of the voting power in favour of issuing shares. Typically, the merger would require approval from a simple majority of 51%. That leaves them needing just an additional 11% to pass. If approved, the merger will close fully, leaving Asset Entities shareholders with just 5.8% of the combined company. Strive is, of course, much larger. The good news is that this is Asset Entities' window to become a public Bitcoin Treasury. They still hold about $750 million from the PIPE financing, and a Section 351 exchange is planned. Cole recently took to X to post about the upcoming vote, recommending that shareholders vote ‘yes.’

On the flip side, if the vote doesn't pass, the merger won’t close. It would leave Asset Entities back on its own again with its current small-scale business and a very limited cash reserve. Given that fragile balance sheet, I expect any failure to merge would put significant pressure on Asset Entities and the stock. I see shares moving negatively if the vote doesn’t pass. However, with 40% of the vote already locked in, management only needs a modest margin of additional support. I expect the merger to get approved, and we should see Asset Entities transition from a small marketing company with under $3 million in assets to a Bitcoin Treasury play with over $1 billion in crypto.

A Position In Bitcoin

If the merger were to go through, how well-positioned are Asset Entities to capitalize on a Bitcoin position? Well, the company plans a Section 351 Exchange, which would immediately transfer Bitcoin into the new entity and make it one of the top 100 corporate Bitcoin treasuries. At that point, we would see Bitcoin as the primary asset on the company's balance sheet next quarter, not cash flow from a business. Their main lever would, of course, be the value of Bitcoin itself. So profitability would have depended on Bitcoin going up. At that point, similar to other treasuries, the stock price will fluctuate based on the value of the crypto the treasury holds. We know they hold about $750 million for the PIPE, so they're ready to accumulate Bitcoin aggressively should the price be right. We may even see them issue debt or equity in the future, similar to what MicroStrategy (MSTR) has done in the past to buy more crypto. The CFO of Strive recently stated on X that the company is ready to start buying crypto as soon as the voting is passed.

So, at current Bitcoin prices of $110,000, the company could assume a total of 13,636 Bitcoin, given it has $750 million from the PIPE and an additional $750 million from warrant exercises. And with a current market cap of just $102 million, we would see the company’s value multiply sharply. I modeled the company's valuation against future Bitcoin price movements to get a sense of the valuation we could see in the near term. Remember, Bitcoin is down from those ~$120,000 highs not so long ago, so an entry point in the near term would see its average Bitcoin price fairly low. If the vote goes through on the 9th, I would expect Asset Entities to open a position fairly quickly.

Valuation

Valuation here is quite tricky to pinpoint since the company is going through such a transitional period. Asset Entities holds a market cap of roughly $102 million, with an enterprise value of just $100 million and no debt. On top of this, their current revenue stream is extremely small, just $344,000 in 1G25 and an annualized figure of $0.7 million. So at current levels, they look highly overvalued compared to that peer group sector. But keep in mind that the sector is formed of communication services companies, and if this merger goes through, Asset Entities will become a full Bitcoin treasury. As of now, on a trailing basis, they hold an EV to sales ratio of 131.06x and a price to sales ratio of 77x against sector medians of just 1.9x and 1.3x, respectively. Its price-to-book ratio of 38x also exceeds the peer group median of just 2x. What this all means is that the market isn't valuing Asset Entities as a small digital marketing business anymore. The probability of the merger going through has already been priced in by the market, and with the price of Bitcoin near all-time highs, Asset Entities finds itself quite overvalued. If the merger closes and the PIPE financing plus the Section 351 Bitcoin exchange go through, the combined entity’s balance sheet and growth story will reset entirely.

What's Next?

All eyes should be on the shareholder vote next week on the 9th of September. If you’ve held shares since before July 21st, then you’re eligible to vote. I expect it will pass, and we should see them open a Bitcoin position sometime after.

data.title: SentinelOne: Ignored AI Cybersecurity Play
data.description:
SentinelOne, Inc. (NYSE:S) rallied somewhat after a strong FQ2 earnings report. The cybersecurity company has constantly been overlooked despite strong growth with a shifting focus on cloud and AI. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock, especially after the recent double bottom around $16 leading into the strong quarter.

Rebounding ARR

SentinelOne reported another strong quarter with growth exceeding 20%. The stock traded near the multi-year lows due to fears regarding net new ARR growth.

The cybersecurity company reported a very disappointing $28 million addition to net new ARR for FQ1 after producing $50+ million in the prior quarters. SentinelOne added $53 million in new net ARR during FQ2 to top $1 billion in total ARR now.

The company actually reported a record net new ARR in FQ2 after hitting only $44 million in the July quarter last year. Net new ARR actually grew 18% YoY.

SentinelOne has promising cloud and AI software, including Purple AI. The AI security product reduced major security events by 60% and led to a 338% ROI leading to the following promising statement from the CEO on the FQ2 earnings call:

Purple AI continues to outperform our expectations, emerging as a key driver of our quarterly results. At its 1-year mark since general availability, Purple AI achieved record momentum in Q2, growing triple digits with attach rates surpassing 30% of licenses sold during the quarter, the highest level to date.

The addition of the Prompt Security product adds more functionality for secure adoption of genAI. The growing proliferation of Agentic AI and AI assistants in enterprises will require new tools to prevent data leakage and misuse before the problems escalate with real-time AI visibility and AI attack prevention, amongst other tools. Currently, Prompt is listed as a leading enterprise genAI product.

While SentinelOne has tons of opportunity in the AI space with Purple AI and now Prompt Security, the stock seemed to have slumped on the appearance of not taking enough business from the CrowdStrike (CRWD) IT outage last year. Either way, SentinelOne is actually growing faster than CrowdStrike now while one is beloved by the market and the other is hated.

SentinelOne has made another transformation in the last few quarters moving to a positive operating margins. The company hit 2.2% operating margins in FQ2 and guided to a strong 4% margin in FQ3 with revenues jumping to $256 million, already topping a $1 billion annual run rate.

Big Multiple Disconnect

CrowdStrike has clearly been a cybersecurity leader, but the disconnect in the valuation multiples is unsustainable. SentinelOne now trades at only 6x forward sales while CrowdStrike trades above 22x.

In essence, SentinelOne would have to more than triple in order to reach the same multiple as CrowdStrike. The more likely outcome is meeting in the middle with a forward P/S multiple in the 10x to 15x range.

The company even ended FQ2 with a cash balance of $1.2 billion and is starting to throw off positive cash flows, allowing for spending $180 million on Prompt Security without a blink. All of the reported GAAP losses are due to non-cash charges for stock-based compensation and amortization of acquired intangible assets.

The stock is cheap at 6x forwards sales for a cybersecurity business and definitely for the current environment and strong position in AI security with Purple AI. The rumored Palo Alto Networks (PANW) deal was at a market valuation of $8 to $10 billion and the company reporting record FQ2 net ARR bookings should boost the normal valuation range for the stock now under such a scenario.

Naturally, the cybersecurity business is hyper competitive. SentinelOne competes in the endpoint security market against CrowdStrike and with many additional specialists in cloud, AI, etc. At any time, a company can lose competitive advantage or run into an issue where products cause IT outages.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that SentinelOne is far too cheap here compared to cybersecurity peers despite growing the business at a solid 22% clip. The stock recently hit a double bottom providing a further strong entry point and a solid risk/reward setup with a few dollars of downside risk and solid upside gain potential with the strong 20% growth and potential multiple expansion in the hot cybersecurity space.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Gross Margin Near 5-Year Low And Can Drop More
data.description:
SMCI stock: FQ4 earnings reveal margin pressure

My last coverage on Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) was published on July 18 with an article titled “Super Micro Computer: Liquid Cooling Can Become Commoditized (Rating Downgrade).” The article rated the stock as a hold due to my concern about the prospects of its liquid cooling racks. Since then, the company has reported its earnings report (ER) for FQ4 in early August. For readers unfamiliar with the numbers, the next chart summarizes the highlights of its financials. Revenue growth slowed to 8% YOY, while CFO (cash from operations) in F4Q was healthy at $864 million, as seen.

What really caught my attention was the gross margin (GM), which came in at 9.6% only for the quarter. The GM thus translates into a margin compression of about 10 bps (basis points) compared to the previous quarter and of about 70 bps compared to the same period last year. Expanding the horizon, as shown in the second chart below, its current margin is among the lowest levels in at least 5 years. Looking ahead, I see several headwinds that could keep pressuring its GM. In the subsequent two sections, I will detail the top 2 on my list: the competition pressure and the working capital requirement.

SMCI stock: competition pressure

As argued in my earlier article, several of SMCI’s key product lineups are facing stiff competition, in my view. Judging by the latest developments, I see competitor Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) gaining momentum in AI servers, a key addressable market for SMCI. It is certainly true that the server market is enjoying a period of rapid growth, and this rising tide could lift all boats. As an example, the chart below shows how significant the growth has been in the worldwide server market. The total revenue almost doubled in 2024 (an increase of 91%, from $40,480 million in Q4 2023 to $77,321 million in Q4 2024).

Dell Technologies and Super Micro are both in the first tier of this booming market and claim the top 2 spots. Dell’s market share is slightly ahead of SMIC (7.2% vs. 6.5% as of Q4 2024), both closely followed by other players. The key differentiator here is Dell’s much higher GM, as shown in the second chart below. DELL’s quarterly gross profit margin has also been under some pressure lately due to various factors (competition intensification, trade disruptions, etc.), as seen. However, Dell's gross profit margin consistently remained higher than Super Micro's. The latest report shows Dell's GM to be 18.7%, approximately doubling SMCI’s margin of 9.45%. With the space becoming more and more crowded, I believe Dell’s thicker margin puts it in a far better competitive position than SMCI to absorb cost and pricing uncertainties.

SMCI stock: working capital and inventory

The second key factor for my concern over SMCI’s GM forward involves its working capital requirements, especially its inventory. Reading into its financial statement, my model shows that inventory played a key role in its margin compression in the past quarter. As you can see from its balance sheet below, inventory increased substantially to $4,680 million in the quarter, translating into a QOQ increase of more than 20% compared to the level of $3,870 million in the past quarter.

Looking ahead, I fear that its elevated inventory could persist due to inventory reserves for older-generation products. For example, some customers could choose to wait for the release of Nvidia's next-generation GPUs, such as the B300/GB300 chips. Besides NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) is also likely to continue to introduce new AI chips with step-function changes in functionality. As such, a logical decision from many of SMCI’s potential customers would be to just delay their orders for as long as their schedule allows to obtain the best functionality for their money (which is a large sum with the price tags of these chips and racks nowadays).

These uncertainties would put pressure on SMCI to manage its working capital efficiently. For a company like Super Micro, two of the key components of its working capital are accounts receivable and inventory. The company needs to navigate these uncertainties to ensure that it does not overbuild older-generation racks and/or hoard inventory. Either case could put a constraint on its capital allocation, limit its ability to scale production, and pressure its profit margins. Judging by its latest financials, I am not sure if SMCI is managing its working capital efficiently. As aforementioned, its GM has dropped to a multi-year low. In the meantime, its working capital has experienced a sharp increase in recent quarters, as you can see from the chart below. In 2025 alone, its working capital has surged from approximately $7 billion in January to the current peak level of almost $10 billion.

Other risks and final thoughts

There are certainly some positives here. After all, I am arguing for a hold rating on the stock instead of a sell rating. As aforementioned, the biggest upside risk I see here is the rapid growth of the overall AI server market. The growth potential is further brightened by the recent “AI Sovereign” policy change made by the Trump administration. At least conceptually, if the total addressable market expands, Super Micro can sell more services even if its market share stagnates or decreases, which can bring about more pronounced economies of scale and provide support for its profit margins.

Also, in terms of valuation, the multiples seem reasonable either by absolute or relative standards. As a reflection, the stock has an overall valuation grade of "B." Its FWD P/E on a non-GAAP basis currently stands at 15.62, a very reasonable ratio in my model in absolute terms, considering its positioning in the sector. Note that its current P/E ratios are also quite close to its historical averages, with a difference of around 5% only. By horizontal comparison, its P/E ratios are significantly discounted from the sector median by as much as 32% in terms of FWD P/E.

All told, this SMCI update is triggered mainly by its FQ4 2025 ER. The earnings are mostly healthy, but we are concerned about the margin pressure. The GM of 9.6% only for FQ4 is a multi-year low, and we unfortunately see large odds for further compression due to competition intensification and also the working capital requirements. These developments lead us to see a neutral risk/return ratio and reiterate our hold rating.

data.title: Yields Up To 7%-12%: 3 REITs To Buy Today
data.description:
REIT share prices (VNQ) have steadily declined for the past three years as interest rates surged, resulting in some of the highest yields in many years.

It's not uncommon to now find REITs yielding 8% or more. In the most extreme cases, you can find REITs yielding as much as 12%.

Typically, such high yields are a clear red flag. It generally means that the market is suspicious about the sustainability of the dividend, and in the case of REITs, such high-yielders are often overleveraged, poorly managed, and own risky assets.

But there are exceptions.

In today's article, I am going to highlight three REITs yielding up to 12% that I think will manage to sustain and even grow their dividend over time.

They each have risks, of course, which I will also discuss below, but they offer attractive risk-to-reward for high-yield seeking investors as part of a well-diversified portfolio.

Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH)

Armada Hoffler Properties is not liked by the market, and I understand why. On the surface, it seems to be a small, diversified REIT with a development-heavy business, some office exposure, a poor dividend track record, and more leverage than average.

A lot of red flags, and it will cause a lot of investors to stop their research there. As a result, it trades at an exceptionally low valuation of just 7x FFO, an estimated 40% discount to NAV, and a 7.7% dividend yield, despite being well covered with a low 55% payout ratio.

That's the valuation of a low-quality REIT with low-quality assets, poor management, and a risky balance sheet.

But we don't see it that way, and this is why we think that AHH is today offering a great opportunity for high-yield seeking investors.

Let's start with the portfolio.

The REIT market dislikes REITs that invest across the board in many different property sectors because you cannot be a jack of all trades and expect to earn superior returns. AHH is typically classified as a diversified REIT because it has exposure to many property sectors, which is hurting its market sentiment.

However, we think that this perception is wrong. AHH is not a diversified REIT. It is a "mixed-use REIT." It owns mostly properties that combine many different uses under one roof, typically in highly urban, walkable locations. It's an asset class of its own that enjoys superior fundamentals, as there are lots of synergies in combining these uses under one roof: Residential, retail, and office.

These properties have consistently enjoyed strong rent growth and high occupancy rates, including even the office component of their portfolio.

They are true Class A "trophy" properties, as the management describes them.

What about the management? It's getting lots of criticism today because the REIT has suffered ever since the beginning of the pandemic in 2020. It came into the crisis with too much leverage and many development projects, which then forced it to cut the dividend twice.

But up until then, the REIT actually had a very strong track record, strongly outperforming the broader REIT market:

The management is also one of the biggest shareholders of the REIT, aligning interests with those of shareholders.

Finally, while its balance sheet is somewhat more heavily leveraged than your typical REIT, with 7x debt-to-EBITDA, this is expected to come back down rapidly over the coming years as it finishes its development projects.

So overall, I think that the REIT is of much higher quality than what the market gives it credit for.

Its portfolio is enjoying steady organic growth, and its development projects should boost that as they come online in the coming years. The management had done well until the recent black swans, and the leverage is expected to come down in the coming years.

The market did not like the recent dividend cut, but it will help them organically reinvest in growth and deleverage the balance sheet.

With a low 55% payout ratio, the 7.7% yielding dividend appears sustainable.

We think that fair value is closer to 12x FFO, which could result in nearly 100% upside.

There are two catalysts for this.

First, the deleveraging. Secondly, the Fed cutting interest rates. Right now, the debt market is pricing in 125 basis points worth of cuts within a year from now, which could lead to significant upside over the coming years:

Global Medical REIT (GMRE)

Global Medical REIT is another one that's not getting much love from the market.

REITs have gone through a multi-year bear market, and GMRE has dropped even more than the rest because it is a small-cap with higher leverage.

It also recently cut its dividend, which does not help its cause.

But I actually think that the REIT is a lot better than what it gets credit for. It owns mostly medical office buildings, which are today undersupplied, and enjoys steady rent growth.

Moreover, this recent cut makes it a lot more attractive.

At the new dividend level, the payout ratio is just 74%, leaving ample cash flow to organically reinvest in growth and pay off some of its debt.

Yet, the dividend yield is still very attractive at 8%.

The yield is only so high because its valuation is so low, trading at just 9x FFO. The management just recently announced a share buyback plan to take advantage of it, and expects to fund it organically with retained cash flow.

This should be highly accretive given how low the valuation has gotten.

The leverage is relatively high at 7.5x debt-to-EBITDA, and therefore, I would not expect much growth until rates are cut.

But as noted earlier, the market expects significant cuts already within a year from now, which could lead to a strong acceleration in growth. Just like AHH, up until the recent bear market, GMRE had a strong track record of outperforming the market.

We estimate fair value at 12x FFO, which would result in nearly 30-40% upside from here. Rents keep growing, and you get paid generously while you wait.

NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP)

Finally, the highest yielder of the group.

I just recently highlighted it in a separate article that you can read by clicking here, so I will keep this short.

I just want to share a highlight from the recent earnings calls. NLCP is a REIT that focuses on cannabis cultivation facilities, which is a riskier property sector, and as a result, the market has priced it at a high 12.4% dividend yield.

But there are many risk-mitigating factors that make this REIT a lot safer than the market appears to understand:

Its properties are leased on a triple net basis, which results in steady cash flow from long-term leases with annual rent escalations and no landlord responsibilities.

It focuses on limited license jurisdictions with growing demand but limited supply, and its properties generally enjoy strong rent coverage.

Finally, it maintains a net cash positive balance sheet, which is truly unique for a REIT. It has practically no debt and access to significant liquidity to be in a position of strength when it faces occasional lease defaults which are inevitable in this business.

The REIT has steadily hiked its dividend since going public, and when asked about it, here's what their CEO said on their most recent conference call:

"Our second quarter dividend of $0.43 per share represented an AFFO payout ratio of 79%, just below our targeted range of 80% to 90%. Our portfolio has remained resilient through a difficult period for the cannabis sector, though we recognize we are not immune to the ongoing financial pressures faced by some industry participants."

So yes, this property sector is riskier than most others, but I still think that the dividend is sustainable at these levels.

They have a low payout ratio, no debt, and enjoy strong rent escalations, which provide significant margin of safety for when they face setbacks.

This is arguably one of the safest 12.4% yielding stocks of the entire market.

Closing Note

High-yield investing can be very risky.

Most of these stocks are priced at high yields for a good reason. They are overleveraged, poorly managed, and face declining cash flows.

Studies show that such companies generally underperform over time.

But if you're selective and know what you're doing, you may find rare high-yielding opportunities that offer generous income and significant upside potential.

That's exactly what we are seeking at High Yield Landlord.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: QDTE's 39% Yield: Income Dream Or Investor Nightmare?
data.description:
Covered call ETFs (XYLD) have exploded in popularity in recent years, with J.P. Morgan rolling out JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), and Neos rolling out Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) and NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), along with several other companies entering the space and seeing sharp increases in assets under management. The YieldMax ETFs (YMAX) have also become extremely popular with investors, offering sky-high yields and weekly payouts.

While the promise of huge yields and high-frequency distributions—along with owning underlying portfolios that include exposure to leading mega-cap stocks that otherwise would not generate anything in the way of yield—certainly has its appeal to income investors, the recent strong performance in mega-cap stocks has also boosted the performance of these covered call funds. This has given investors the impression that their huge yields are sustainable and that they can generate outsized total returns that, of late, have even outperformed leading traditional dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

However, beneath the shiny yields, these funds contain structural flaws that position them to eventually disappoint income investors. I deem their NAVs and their payouts unsustainable and expect them to significantly underperform their underlying holdings, and likely the broader market and even dividend growth stocks, over the long term. In this article, I will explain why the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF's (BATS:QDTE) payout and NAV look especially unsustainable and why we avoid it.

What QDTE Offers on the Surface

QDTE seeks to track what it deems the Innovation 100 Index, which closely mirrors the NASDAQ 100 Index (QQQ). In addition to its underlying exposure, it uses a synthetic deep-in-the-money long-call strategy, and then it sells zero-DTE out-of-the-money call options daily to generate consistent income.

This income is then distributed to shareholders on a weekly basis and is typically classified as a return of capital due to the way the fund is structured. Additionally, it charges investors a 0.97% expense ratio. This is fairly high when compared to other covered call ETFs like JEPI and JEPQ, which charge just a 0.35% expense ratio, and even QQQI and SPYI charge considerably lower expense ratios at 0.68%.

However, there is considerable appeal that QDTE offers. In particular, the weekly distributions and the 38.9% trailing 12-month dividend yield look very attractive compared to what other covered call funds offer. For example, JEPI and JEPQ currently only offer between 8.5% and 11.3% trailing 12-month distribution yields. So you get between three and four times as much from QDTE in terms of income from distributions as you do with those other funds. Additionally, JEPI and JEPQ only pay monthly, whereas QDTE pays weekly.

Why QDTE’s Yield Is Misleading

While that would seem to provide a case-closed, airtight argument in favor of QDTE, there is much more going on here that deserves attention. For example, QDTE’s price per share has materially lagged JEPQ and JEPI since inception.

Meanwhile, even though its yield is so much higher and it has delivered solid returns since its inception, its overall total return has not performed particularly impressively compared to JEPQ and QQQI, indicating that this extra yield it is generating is 100% coming out of its stock price and is not adding any extra value.

Therefore, while the structure of the fund—with a strategy designed to generate massive and high-frequency distributions—looks appealing on the surface, much of it appears to be nothing more than taking money from one bucket and putting it in another instead of actually creating additional value.

This then calls into question the excessive yield being paid. Even more concerning is the significant NAV erosion of the fund, with it losing over 25% of its NAV per share since its launch a little over a year ago. What this indicates is that its yield is not sustainable either and that eventually, as its NAV continues to plummet, its distributions will inevitably follow suit as there will simply be less capital to generate income from. What this effectively turns into is paying extra money to a fund manager to give you your own money back, which makes little sense.

Why Other Covered Call ETFs Look Better by Comparison

I already have enough of an issue with JEPQ and QQQI because their covered call strategies put them at risk of being left hopelessly behind their underlying indexes during periods of sharp market declines followed by a quick V-shaped recovery. Moreover, their higher expense ratios relative to a lower-cost underlying fund such as the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) or even just QQQ further erode returns.

QDTE puts that structural flaw on steroids with an even higher expense ratio, even more NAV erosion, and further capping of upside performance. This all begs the question of why even invest in QDTE in the first place if it is an inferior total return vehicle to simply investing in the underlying index with a much lower expense ratio.

While its yield is massive, the sustainability of those payouts and the sustainability of its underlying NAV are both significantly inferior to those of JEPQ and QQQI. On top of that, you are paying a much higher expense ratio for QDTE than for those other options. Over the long term, I would expect QQQI and JEPQ to outperform QDTE as well.

Investor Takeaway

Given the facts laid out in this article, I see no scenario in which QDTE is worth owning, because you either want to have a sustainable, efficient income source to fund your retirement, or you want to have a fund that has maximum long-term total return potential to accumulate wealth as you work toward retirement. In either scenario, QDTE falls short relative to alternatives. As a result, we have shunned funds like this in favor of sustainable and growing, yet still attractive, high yields in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Bank of America Floating Preferred Comes With A 4% Floor
data.description:
Introduction

Besides fixed-rate and floating-rate preferred stocks, there is a hybrid version, such as the Bank of America Corporation PFD DP1/1200 4 (BML.PR.J), whose coupon floats but also has a minimum fixed rate. After COVID hit, that feature provided a nice coupon floor for investors, as the next chart indicates.

Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) actually has four preferred stocks in the BML (originally Merrill Lynch) series. This one has the best floor and floating component, thus why it is the one reviewed. While I consider this issue unlikely to suffer from non-payments or default, the low yield results in a Hold rating. With a little work, other preferred stocks with the same credit rating can be found.

Bank of America Corporation review

Understanding the issuer is an important part of a preferred stock’s due diligence process. Seeking Alpha describes this bank as (edited):

Bank of America Corporation, through its subsidiaries, provides various financial products and services for individual consumers, small and middle-market businesses, institutional investors, large corporations, and governments worldwide. The company operates through four segments. Consumer Banking offers traditional and money market savings accounts, certificates of deposit and IRAs, checking accounts, and investment accounts and products. Global Wealth & Investment Management provides investment management, and trust and retirement products. Global Banking offers lending products and services, including commercial loans, leases, commitment facilities, trade finance, and commercial real estate and asset-based lending; treasury solutions, and fixed-income and equity research services. Global Markets provides market-making, financing, securities clearing, settlement, and custody services; securities and derivative products; and risk management products. Bank of America Corporation was founded in 1784 and is based in Charlotte, North Carolina.

I like to measure the default risk for the preferred by dividing the Total Common equity by the value of all the preferred stock outstanding, data found on the balance sheet.

Here the ratio is still almost 12X even with the value of the Common stock down 60% since 2019.

Bank of America Corporation PFD DP1/1200 4 review

Even though this preferred stock was originally issued by Merrill Lynch, like most bank issues, the dividends are non-cumulative. As stated, this issue has a 4% minimum coupon and now uses SOFR in place of LIBOR and adds an additional 26.161bps to compensate for the fact that SOFR has less risk than the old LIBOR. The issue has the lowest investment-grade rating (BBB-) and qualifies for the 15% tax rate on its dividends.

Comparing similar preferred stocks

To help readers decide, here are several other preferreds with 4% coupon floors.

  • Morgan Stanley Floating Rate Dep Shares Series A Non-cumul Preferred Stock (MS.PR.A): 70bps fixed + 3-mo SOFR + 26.161bps
  • Goldman Sachs Group, Dep Shares Float Rate Non-cumul Preferred Stock, Series C (GS.PR.C): 75bps fixed + 3-mo SOFR + 26.161bps

I decided to include this fixed-rate security where the coupon approximates the current floating rates of the others, the Bank of America Corp., 5.0% Dep Shares Non-cumul Preferred Stock Series LL (BAC.PR.N).

Factor BML J MS A GS C BAC N
Issued 11-15-05 6-26-06 4-18-05 9-10-19
Est. Coupon* 5.46% 5.28% 5.37% 5.00%
Fixed add-on (bps) 75 70 75 NA
Price $21.22 $21.88 $21.44 $20.74
Yield 6.43% 6.15% 6.21% 5.92%
Call date 11-28-10 7-15-11 10-31-10 9-17-24
Rating BBB- BBB- BB+ BBB-

* First three issues reset each quarter using the formula listed prior. Call date determines reset calendar. Currently the 3-mo SOFR is around 4.2%.

Analysis

  • Because the three floaters reset on different dates, their coupons could differ by larger or smaller margins than what the formula would imply, as is now the case.
  • All four issues have no Call protection.
  • Amongst this set, everything else being equal, the MS PFD would be Called last versus the other floaters.
  • A SOFR rate near 4% is roughly the dividing line concerning BAC being Called. A higher than 4% SOFR should make BAC last in line; below that, it would have the highest coupon.
  • To get a better Call probability, each coupon should be compared to the other preferred stocks from that issuer.

Conclusion

With other financial firms offering higher yields for similarly rated preferreds, I am giving the BML a Hold rating.

Portfolio strategy

Even after deciding to invest in individual preferred stocks, there are decisions to be made around the different features to pick from, some of which include the following:

  • Fixed or floating coupon: While fixed provided greater income certainty, floating provides better price protection when rates are climbing. As covered here, is there a coupon floor?
  • Perpetual or Term: Are you okay knowing the issue either has a termination date or mandatory conversion date? If not, perpetually makes more sense.
  • Call protection/risk: There are preferred stocks without a call date, though rare. For the rest, the decision is how long you want to be protected from that possibility.
  • Default/bankruptcy protection: The capital ranking (bonds, preferreds, common) comes into play based on one's risk tolerance.
  • Cumulative or not: Some preferreds come with a cumulative feature requiring dividends to be current if the company wants to pay a common stock dividend.

Call risk can be minimized by owning the lowest coupon issued by that company, as it should be last in line to get Called. If a non-cumulative issue, a high-yielding common stock issuer has less payment-skipping risk as they would be required to stop the common dividend while the preferred stock(s) are not paying, an event that would tank the price of the common stock.

data.title: Chipotle: Higher-Quality Food And Overseas Expansion Could Offset Sales Deceleration
data.description:
Thesis

I rate Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) as a Buy. I expect 20% upside for the next 12 months.

While I primarily assume these were just two bad quarters, and I think the company has somewhat above-average quality products, we should be cautious about the price-to-quality relation.

Additionally, the overseas expansion seems very promising. They are expanding into Canada, Mexico, Europe, and the Middle East, and the results they're getting are pretty good. So, a potentially saturated market in the U.S. may not be the end of a growth story.

Business Overview and Latest Results

Chipotle Mexican Grill is a well-known restaurant chain that offers a variety of Mexican dishes, including burritos, burrito bowls, quesadillas, tacos, and salads.

Regarding its core business, the company offers fast food of higher-than-average quality but without the downsides usually associated with it. This means they aim to have the following characteristics: high-quality food, a 'visible assembly line' process, allowing customers to see how their food is being prepared, and mostly organic food. Their "Food with Integrity" program prioritizes animal welfare; it doesn't allow added hormones and non-therapeutic antibiotics. Also, Chipotle's internal teams and third-party auditors conduct both scheduled and unannounced inspections of their suppliers' farms. They avoid using artificial preservatives and colorings. Additionally, they don't use freezers; they transport the meat in refrigerated trucks, typically around 33-36°F (0-2°C). Lastly, something to add here is that they diverted 50% of their waste from landfills and are committed to reducing their greenhouse gas emissions by 50% by 2030.

Now, most of these characteristics imply a higher cost than average. Taco Bell, a lower-cost Mexican food chain, has menus that average $8 to $12 (similar to non-Mexican foods such as McDonald's and Burger King), while the average Chipotle meal may cost between $12 and $16.

So, the question is, is this higher-quality approach worth it for the client to pay above average? Setting aside the subjective approach (which may be valid), let's analyze these characteristics in more detail from a more objective standpoint.

First, let's consider added hormones. While in Europe, they are prohibited, in the USA, they are allowed with restrictions. A meta-analysis published in 2018 on hormonal growth promotants (HGPs) found that HGPs improve production efficiency but may increase the toughness of beef.

Second, let's consider the no-freezer policy. Fresh meat, which hasn't been frozen, generally has a firmer texture and better flavor. When meat is frozen, ice crystals form inside muscle cells and can break down fibers, which often results in meat that is tougher, less juicy, and sometimes less flavorful once thawed and cooked. However, commercial flash freezing, which freezes meat very quickly, minimizes this damage and can preserve quality very well, making the differences in taste and texture less noticeable.

Third, avoiding artificial preservatives and colorings is generally considered a healthier approach, primarily because it avoids potentially harmful chemicals and additives. While a wide range of chemicals are used, we can't lump them all together and state that all of them are bad. But as a rule of thumb, it's a good idea to avoid them.

So, to wrap up, the quality of food may be somewhat above average. Even though much of the evidence isn't set in stone, some clues indicate that their food may be of better quality than that of an average fast-food company.

Regarding the latest results, as we already know, this quarter and the first half of the year (the last two quarters) have been poor. While total revenues grew 3% Y/Y on a quarterly basis, 'comp sales' (which exclude new restaurant sales) are down by 4%, and the number of transactions is decreasing at an even lower rate of 4.9% Y/Y.

One of my peers wrote a pretty good article and delved deep into a historical comparison that I highly recommend. For obvious reasons, I will not delve into something that is already well covered. But the key factor for us is to decide whether these one and two quarters were just bad ones, or if they represent a trend change to slower growth.

Outlook and Overseas Expansion

The fast-food market is expected to grow at a 5% CAGR until 2029, according to Grand View Research, and 7.65% between 2020 and 2030, according to Mordor Intelligence. Also, according to Mordor, the main drivers of growth are online platforms that improve accessibility and reach, and the increased urban lifestyles that demand quick and convenient meal solutions. The main restraints on growth are increased health concerns, rising regulatory scrutiny, and the rising demand for natural and minimally processed ingredients.

However, as I discussed in the company overview section, this is not just a regular fast-food company. Their higher scrutiny in preparing food, in a more natural and enhanced way, not only provides them with immunity to those restraints of growth but also empowers them to continue growing at an above-average industry rate. So, we may expect rates above average for this company.

Additionally, the company has multiple initiatives to expand overseas. In Canada, they almost tripled the number of restaurants over the last 5 years, reaching up to 61, which implies a CAGR of around 24%. Also, this kind of growth reflects the quality of the product they're offering. They also mentioned expanding into London and Germany. In Kuwait and Dubai, they already have 5 restaurants, and their franchisee, the Alshaya Group, plans to open new restaurants in the second half of 2025. It appears that they are doing pretty well, as highlighted in the last earnings call:

Our restaurant in the Avenues Mall in Kuwait completed its first year of operations, with revenue surpassing the average unit volume in the United States.

Lastly, in early 2026, they will open the first restaurant in Mexico. This presents a two-sided issue. On the one hand, it could be tricky to enter the Mexican market and offer burritos, given that many people prepare them at home. As a result, burritos are often tailor-made to personal taste, and consumers may be much more discerning. In other words, they may be much more picky. On the other hand, their strong suit is the higher-quality fast food, which may be appealing to a more specific clientele. It will be interesting to see how this strategy unfolds.

Valuation

Relative valuation, which is based on a forward earnings model, suggests the company is fairly valued when compared to other restaurant stocks.

However, I should be explicit about what my reasonable assumptions are for this company. I assume that revenues will grow 12% in the next 10 years; this may be optimistic, but reasonable. The main drivers of this are: overseas expansion and a higher rate than conventional fast food companies, due to the higher quality offered. Also, the net margin would reach 18% in the tenth year, since the company has fixed costs, and with revenues growing at the low teens, which I think is attainable. Also, I'm assuming a P/E ratio of 25 as normal for this company and a share reduction of 1% every year. In the past three fiscal years, they repurchased 1.08% on average; this could be conservative if they manage to increase profitability in a few years, so they would be able to repurchase at a higher rate. However, my goal here is to be reasonable in order to value the company.

Risks

While there are several risks, I will highlight the two that I consider critical and a third, more minor one.

First, comp sales (sales excluding new restaurants in the last 12 months) are falling. Total sales are decelerating. We don't know the future, but there are two options here: the company just had one or two bad quarters, and that's all. Or, on the contrary, the long-term growth trend has ended, and we are entering a slow-growth regime. As for me, I think that every company has bad quarters, but sooner or later it returns to its average performance. However, only time will tell.

Second, there are operating risks. The company experienced foodborne illness outbreaks in the past, with cases of E. coli and norovirus from 2015 to 2018. The company's image is critical for future success.

The third, a minor risk, is the following. The company tried to market the idea that they make handmade, authentic Mexican food; however, with the latest initiatives of increasing technology in the processes to improve efficiency, this can jeopardize the previous idea. However, I believe that people are pragmatic, and they don't care much about the dichotomy between 'machines/technology' and 'handmade food.' Instead, they may worry about quality and healthy food. Having said that, I state this just as a minor risk and as something to think about.

Conclusion

I believe that the company is offering somewhat above-average food, but not to a great extent. Also, I think that every company has bad quarters, and due to this, we can expect some reversion to the mean.

Something to be aware of is that summer quarters are usually the best. The third quarter is critical because if they don't perform well in the summer, when winter comes, we will be in a more difficult situation. As for me, I believe these were just a couple of bad quarters, and we may expect a reversion to the mean. It's also worth noting the price-to-quality relationship in the products. I mean, if products are really being demanded and can be priced at current levels, or if there is some demand issue. Having said that, I rate Chipotle Mexican Grill as a buy.

data.title: Navigating Retirement With Confidence With Yields Over 9%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

If you want to drive somewhere today, you’re likely to pull up Google Maps or Apple Maps to navigate to your destination. The idea of relying on a navigation aid isn’t new. Over a thousand years ago, the magnetic compass transformed exploration, enabling sailors to venture far from familiar territories with confidence. This is a simple device that always pointed north and guided thousands of voyagers through calm waters and rough seas, centuries before satellites could scan the earth. Technology has advanced, yet our modern navigation systems still rest on the same principle of orientation and direction that the simple magnetic compass first offered.

In investing, certain income-producing assets serve a similar role, providing predictability and stability through ever-changing circumstances. Midstream infrastructure, for example, functions like a toll road for energy, generating steady, fee-based revenues regardless of market and commodity price swings. Preferred shares, likewise, have been relied upon by financial firms for decades as a hybrid of debt and equity. These present resilient instruments designed to maintain their income-generation through market volatility.

Just as the magnetic compass guided explorers across uncharted seas, these proven income-focused sectors will continue to orient investors toward financial stability, no matter how unpredictable the journey may seem. Let us now discuss our top picks from these two sectors.

Pick #1: WES – Yield 9.4%

Western Midstream (WES) is a midstream company that owns and operates over 14,000 miles of pipeline, 21 gathering systems, and 77 processing and treating facilities across key energy-producing regions in America, namely the Powder River Basin, DJ Basin, and Delaware Basin. 95% and 100% of WES's natural gas and liquids contracts, respectively, are Fee-Based, ensuring predictable revenues through commodity price swings.

WES market price has had post-earnings weakness, likely due to the company’s planned acquisition of Aris Water Solutions for $1.5 billion in an equity and cash transaction. However, Mr. Market overlooks the fact that this bolt-on acquisition almost doubles WES's existing produced-water business, which comprises 830 miles of pipeline and a total disposal capacity of 2,035 MBbls/d. After the close of this transaction, WES's pro forma produced-water disposal capacity will exceed 3.8 million barrels per day.

Aris's assets extend WES's existing footprint in the Permian basin at a time when this largest and most productive oil basin in the United States is generating higher water volumes due to its rising gas-to-oil ratio. The lucrativeness is comparable to selling shovels and pants to folks on their way to Alaska during the gold rush!

Note: WES is a Master Limited Partnership that issues a Schedule K-1 to investors.

WES’ post-market sell-off presents an attractive opportunity to buy the cheapest midstream MLP at cheaper prices. WES reported a robust second quarter, with the highest quarterly adjusted EBITDA ($618 million) in its operating history. The company reported high system operability and activity levels, which resulted in throughput gains in all its assets and product lines, notably natural gas, crude oil, and NGLs, and produced water. The company maintains a BBB-rated balance sheet with $2.1 billion in liquidity (including $130 million in cash).

WES generated $388 million in FCF during the second quarter, or $33 million in FCF after distributions. Management expects 2026 CapEx to be elevated, as the company is advancing two major Delaware Basin projects – the 300 MMcf/d North Loving II processing train (ETA 2Q 2027) backed by strong producer demand and the 800 MBbls/d Pathfinder produced-water pipeline (ETA 1Q 2027) with firm commitments from OXY. These projects expand WES’s processing capacity to 2.5 Bcf/d, moving them closer to the giants in the midstream industry.

Management affirmed the company’s long-term mid-single-digit distribution growth outlook and plan, with a focus on improving cash flow certainty and distribution coverage.

WES is delivering strong results and is quietly growing to join the ranks of the larger midstream companies in America. Currently, buyers can secure a 9.4% yield, with excellent prospects of steady growth over the long term.

Pick #2: JPC – Yield 9.9%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that has dual benefits from rate cuts of any magnitude. ~91% of the fund’s assets are invested in institutional preferreds, and the remaining in retail preferred shares ($25 par). 80% of JPC’s portfolio is investment-grade, implying a strong emphasis on instruments of high credit quality.

JPC is deeply diversified across 262 holdings, with banking (including regional banks), capital markets, and insurance firms representing 82% of the portfolio. Historically, financial services have been the largest issuers of preferred securities, boasting high dividend coverage despite a regulatory requirement to keep them non-cumulative.

JPC operates with 37% leverage to amplify the returns from active management, but this leverage worked against them during rate increases, as seen from the NAV decline.

The CEF borrows at OBFR (Overnight Bank Funding Rate) plus 0.85% per annum on the amounts borrowed. The OBFR typically matches the upper limit of the Fed Funds rate, so a rate cut will immediately reduce interest expenses for JPC.

During FY 2024 (year ended Jan 31, 2025), 73% of JPC’s distributions were supported by Net Investment Income, and this will improve further as the fund’s interest expenses reduce. Moreover, valuations of preferred securities will improve, positioning the fund well for capital gains, and we see a strong case for JPC to raise its distribution as the Fed cuts.

JPC pays monthly distributions that have averaged +90% QDI, and currently yields 9.9%. With its trading prices approximately at par with NAV, JPC presents an attractive investment to wait for and benefit from rate cuts.

Conclusion

Just as the compass provided sailors with direction through uncharted waters and unpredictable weather, resilient income assets that form the basis for a growing economy can provide investors with greater stability and steady cash flows through bull markets, bear markets, and everything in between.

At High Dividend Opportunities, our Income Method transforms savings into a dependable stream of cash flow – designed to withstand both turbulence and tranquility in the markets. This reliable income engine serves as a financial compass, guiding you toward financial independence and a secure retirement on your terms. This is the beauty of our Income Method, and the power of income investing.

data.title: My Top 2 Dividend Picks To Capture $100 Trillion In Secular Growth
data.description:
Introduction

When I was still in college, studying International Business Administration, we were asked an interesting question during what I believe was a Corporate Finance course (I don't remember it exactly).

It was something along the lines of "What's bigger, the global equity market or the global debt market?"

Most people believed the answer was the stock market. I get that. After all, many students had some experience trading stocks, which means most are aware of the massive trading volumes and market caps of the world's biggest companies. Right now, the S&P 500's biggest five stocks have a market capitalization of $16.4 trillion. Again, that's just five stocks...

Nine companies in the index have a market cap of more than $1 trillion, while nearly 400 companies have a market cap of less than $100 billion.

Additionally, the stock market has more than tripled in size over the past ten years, fueled by QE in the past, fiscal stimulus, and rapid tech advancements.

Nonetheless, the global equities are "nothing" compared to the debt market.

Using 2023 data from the London Stock Exchange Group, we see that global markets surpassed $1 quadrillion. That's 1,000 times $1 trillion. Equities made up just $175 trillion. Global rates and credit totaled over $760 trillion, roughly 75% of the entire global market.

There are many reasons for this. For starters, going public (issuing stock) isn't an option for many organizations and individuals in need of funding. For example, virtually all governments, including federal and municipal, require bond issuance to fund their operations and deficits. Then there are many corporations that use bank loans and public bond markets for funding.

It's also often more attractive for large investors like pension funds and insurance companies, who prefer to invest in bonds and private debt, as it lowers their financial risks and overall volatility.

Moreover, funding requirements are often diverse. As credit markets offer a super wide range of instruments like commercial paper, short-term loans, long-term bonds, and hybrid alternatives, they can meet these needs.

For example:

  • If we buy a house, we use a mortgage.
  • If we buy a car, we finance it using a car loan.
  • Struggling consumers may use short-term paycheck loans or BNPL.
  • Most companies are not large enough to access the public debt market.

When adding that the United States and the United Kingdom are quite unusual with regard to the extent that many corporations use the equity market, one can almost make the case that it's surprising that global debt doesn't account for far more than 75% of the global market volume.

With that said, one of the best things is that one person's liability is another person's asset. Bank of America (BAC), for example, had $1.08 trillion in loans and leases going into this year, as we can see below, excluding allowances for bad loans and losses. Painting with a broad brush—all of this is interest-bearing debt that benefits its dividend. On the other side of the equation are companies and individuals paying interest.

When picking the right clients, the debt business is a terrific place to be. I remember when a former banker of one of the world's biggest banks told me how eager they were to expand their car loan business, as the rates on these deals were super juicy. That was roughly eight years ago. It's still true today.

Personally, I'm an equity guy. I don't hold debt, and I don't even have debt outstanding at any bank. I prefer to invest my capital in fascinating companies with attractive business models and the ability to consistently grow their market cap and free cash flow for buybacks and dividends.

However, that doesn't mean I cannot benefit from the biggest asset market in the world. See, as we discussed, there are many ways to invest in debt. One of them is by buying equity in some of the most professional lenders on the market, the ones that don't "willy-nilly" chase income but have the capacity to benefit from the most attractive deals on the market.

That's why I'm telling investors to forget about car loans, mortgages, and consumer debt. Focus on the highest-demand areas with secular growth.

This includes the energy transition, power and utility investments, and digital infrastructure like data centers. Apollo Global Management estimates that these areas alone come with up to $100 trillion in financing demand.

But wait. It gets better!

This isn't just about high demand for financing. It's also about access to capital. As most of you will know, governments all over the world are increasingly dealing with large deficits and elevated debt-to-GDP ratios.

If governments want to modernize their economy (think of Germany), they will have to find ways beyond boosting their own deficit. As a result, there's a strong push for a public-private partnership model, where private capital, like that managed by Apollo, is essential to bridge the funding gap, making them key beneficiaries thanks to their expertise, connections, and balance sheets.

That's one of the reasons why I like these companies.

  • Debt demand is booming.
  • Interest rates may have come down, but they still offer elevated income potential.
  • Large lenders have the expertise and size to win large deals.
  • Especially government organizations will have to rely on public-private partnerships to ease the strain on government finances.

As an "equity guy" with a focus on dividend growth, there are terrific opportunities to make money in a market I expect to deliver tremendous results for many years to come.

That's why I'll use the second part of this article to present you with some of my favorite ideas.

Buying Two Fast-Growing Asset Managers

When it comes to private credit, two companies stand out to me. These are Apollo Global Management (APO) and Blue Owl Capital (OWL). While I also like players like Ares Management Corp. (ARES), these two have everything I'm looking for, including attractive valuations, but more on that later.

Apollo has a clear size benefit. The company, which was founded more than three decades ago, has grown its assets under management ("AUM") to a record $840 million, with fee-generating AUM of $638 million. Even better, close to 60% of its AUM and 75% of its fee-generating AUM is perpetual capital, which means it has no "end-date" and can be deployed long-term.

Moreover, after buying Athene, it now also owns a huge retirement services business that provides it with a steady flow of capital, making Apollo the biggest alternative credit business in the world, the biggest annuity seller in the U.S., and the largest investment-grade alternative credit business.

This business originated $81 billion in assets during 2Q25. As impressive as that may be, the most impressive thing is Apollo's ability to turn this into an advantage by finding value-adding investments. Currently, it has a spread over treasuries of 350 basis points, which provides consistent earnings growth.

Through 2029, it aims to grow its fee-related earnings by 20% per year.

Even better, growth is supported by diversified channels, as the company maintains platforms like ATLAS and MidCap, which had 30% quarter-over-quarter growth, core credit, consisting of CRE debt, large-cap direct lending, fund finance doubling quarter-over-quarter, and high-grade capital solutions that include more than $8 billion across four deals, including AES, BP, Mumbai Airport, and EDF.

According to the company, the EDF transaction, which is a £4.5 billion sterling-denominated financing for U.K. nuclear projects, marked the largest private credit deal in that currency. It shows Apollo's massive footprint in the booming global energy/distribution sector.

However, as we can see above, the bad news is that the dividend has not been a priority in the past. Apollo yields just 1.50% without any meaningful growth in its dividend.

The good news is that its valuation makes up for it, as analysts expect 5% EPS growth in 2025 to be followed by 18% growth in both 2026 and 2027. As APO shares trade at just 17.8x earnings, I see >16% annual returns going forward.

If you are searching for a higher income without having to give up on potentially elevated capital gains, Blue Owl may be perfect for you.

Blue Owl is a company I have brought up way more than Apollo, as I like its Business Development Company, Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), and its focus on data centers. With a market cap of $12 billion, Blue Owl is much smaller than Apollo's market cap of nearly $80 billion. While it somewhat reduces size advantages, it also means that with the right measures, it can grow much faster than its larger peers.

Currently, it has more than $280 billion in assets under management, with roughly half of that coming from credit operations. On top of that, it owns more than 5,700 real estate properties and manages close to $70 billion in strategic capital through minority stakes in other funds and partnerships.

Right now, this business is booming, as fundraising and key financial metrics like fee-related earnings are all growing by elevated double digits:

We raised $14 billion of new capital during the quarter, bringing us to a record capital raise of $55 billion over the last 12 months or 28% of our assets under management a year ago. And these numbers do not yet reflect any meaningful contributions from our acquisitions this past year where we are anticipating significant synergies over the next couple of years. We have grown our FRE revenues by 29%, FRE by 23% and DE by 20% year-over-year on a last 12 months basis, continuing the steady march up and to the right, supported by our substantial permanent capital base. - OWL 2Q25 Earnings Call

One key reason why I am including the company is that, just like Apollo, it's exploiting major secular growth trends. For example, during its 2Q25 call, the company noted that digital infrastructure saw a $7 billion fund close, with rapid deployment into data centers. This positions Blue Owl as a leader with 3.8 gigawatts of leased capacity (that's about 5% of the global capacity).

Moreover, despite current challenges in cyclical sectors (that I expect to turn into tailwinds soon), the company is seeing strong credit metrics, noting that "Credit quality and direct lending remain strong with average annual realized losses at 13 basis points. We are not seeing any meaningful changes in the underlying metrics. This has been a constant theme over the last few years."

With regard to its dividend, the company raised its dividend by 25% this year to $0.225 per share per quarter. This implies a yield of 4.9%, one of the highest numbers among all high-quality asset managers.

While the dividend has a 107% payout ratio based on 2025E EPS (FactSet), the company isn't irresponsible, as it knows that fast growth will provide a lot of room for future dividend growth. That's also why it's great news that the company aims to grow its dividend in line with distributable earnings.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts see 23% and 18% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively. As OWL trades at just 22.3x earnings, below its long-term average of 29.0x, I see a lot of upside, as even an unchanged valuation multiple would still result in a theoretical annual return of more than 18%, including its near-5% dividend.

Even in the past, both APO and OWL have done tremendously well, returning 218% and 113% since the Blue Owl IPO in 2021, easily beating the market.

Going forward, I expect this to continue for two simple reasons:

  • I am very bullish on private credit, especially in areas with secular growth.
  • Apollo and Blue Owl are my two favorite players in this market.

Takeaway

While I'm an equity guy, which I don't think will ever change, I'm not blind to the opportunities in other areas.

As we discussed in this article, I believe private credit is a fantastic place to be, especially in areas that fuel generational trends like the energy transition, data centers, and modern infrastructure.

This is where Apollo and Blue Owl come in.

These aren't banks. No. They are fast-growing asset managers with the expertise and balance sheets to win massive deals and use favorable spreads to turn this into shareholder value.

They also offer a backdoor for equity investors like me to profit from what has become a multi-trillion-dollar debt boom through potential capital appreciation and growing dividends.

All things considered, it's a powerful way to play the biggest market in the world and have a very realistic shot at generating substantial alpha for many years to come.

Risks to My Thesis

The single biggest risk is a recession or any economic environment that causes general debt quality to deteriorate. Regardless of AI and other benefits, this could derail the growth story, as private lenders remain very cyclical assets.

data.title: NIO: Don't Sell The Rally Yet
data.description:
NIO Inc. (NYSE:NIO) submitted a mixed earnings sheet on Tuesday that showed an EPS beat but a revenue miss. NIO generated 9% year-over-year top-line growth and benefited from a continual vehicle margin increase: in the second quarter, the electric vehicle company achieved a vehicle margin of 10.3% (+0.1 PP Q/Q) but continued to suffer from high operating losses in its core business. I continue to see potential for NIO to expand its valuation if the start-up succeeds in narrowing its operating losses while slowly building up its vehicle margins. Since shares of NIO are still very attractively valued, I also see a risk profile that is skewed to the upside.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of NIO a strong buy in my last work on the EV company—Don't Give Up Just Yet—mainly because I saw strong momentum for the firm's low-cost EV brand, Onvo. Since then, shares of the EV start-up have surged ~80%, but I see more upside left if NIO manages to lower its operating losses while maintaining the current rate of delivery growth. Further, NIO submitted a strong delivery outlook for Q3'25, and shares are underpriced relative to other EV start-ups in the industry group.

NIO Reported Mixed Q2 Results

NIO submitted an earnings scorecard for the second quarter on Tuesday that beat on EPS but missed on the top line: NIO reported adjusted earnings of $(0.26) per share, which surpassed the firm's consensus estimate by $0.05 per share. The top line reached $2.66B and fell short of the average prediction by $102.9M.

Despite the revenue estimate miss, NIO delivered strong results in terms of its top line and its vehicle margins in the second quarter. NIO generated a vehicle margin of 10.3% in Q2'25, which reflects a 0.1% Q/Q margin increase. For comparison, XPeng (XPEV), which has overtaken its electric vehicle rivals in terms of delivery growth in recent months, reported a vehicle margin of 14.3% (+3.8 PP Q/Q) in Q2'25, while Li Auto (LI) is still leading the industry group with a vehicle margin of 19.4% (-0.4 PP Q/Q).

Because of higher margins (on both a vehicle and gross profit level) and a higher delivery volume compared to Q2'24, NIO generated 19.0B Chinese Yuan ($2.7B), showing 9% year-over-year growth. NIO did make considerable progress in the second quarter in ramping up its deliveries, mainly due to the company's new brand launches, Onvo and Firefly, which is where the EV company expects its momentum to continue.

In Q2'25, NIO delivered 72,056 electric vehicles, showing 25.6% year-over-year growth. In August, NIO delivered 31,305 EVs, a record monthly delivery volume for the EV start-up, showing 55.2% year-over-year growth. This growth was chiefly driven by Onvo, the firm's low-cost EV brand, which the company launched last year in order to woe more price-conscious younger buyers of EVs. In August, Onvo deliveries totaled 16,434 EVs (+175% month-over-month), representing about 53% of all deliveries during the month. Firefly, which is another low-cost compact EV brand for urban buyers that NIO kicked off in April 2025, is also seeing strong growth: Firefly deliveries totaled 4,346 EVs in August, up 83.7% month-over-month.

Onvo-driven delivery growth is the still the number one reason for me why I continue to recommend shares of NIO—despite a nearly 80% price gain since my last recommendation.

Unfortunately, NIO has one major weakness: the company is losing a ton of money on its EV operations. In the second quarter, NIO generated an operating loss of 4.9B Chinese Yuan ($685.2M), which showed a decline rate of 9% year-over-year, but NIO's losses are still material, especially in comparison to other EV companies. Li Auto, as an example, generated operating income of 827M Chinese Yuan ($115.4M), while XPeng's financial statements showed a loss of 934.6M Chinese Yuan ($130.5M). From a profitability perspective, NIO has the worst setup and needs to urgently improve its margins in order to sustain recent valuation gains.

NIO's Outlook for the Third Quarter

Despite high operating losses, the good news for the EV start-up is that NIO expects strong delivery momentum for Onvo- and Firefly-branded electric vehicles to last into the second half of the year. According to NIO's earnings scorecard, the company expects to see a total delivery volume of between 87,000 and 91,000 vehicles in Q3'25, led by Onvo, which would show an increase of up to 47.1% compared to the year-earlier period.

NIO's Valuation and Catalysts for an Upside Revaluation

One of the single most attractive features of an investment in NIO is that the company is trading at a price-to-revenue ratio, based off of FY 2026 estimates, of less than 1.0X—which makes it an absolute bargain in the EV industry group, in my opinion.

NIO is currently valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.91X, which establishes the EV start-up as the cheapest EV play in the electric vehicle industry group. XPeng, which currently has the strongest momentum in terms of deliveries, is trading at a forward P/S ratio of 1.23X, while Li Auto is still the best deal, in my opinion, given its considerable margin advantage and low P/S ratio of 0.92X.

In my opinion, NIO still has upside revaluation potential as it executes on its Onvo-driven growth strategy. If NIO can sustain its growth momentum in its low-cost EV segment, I believe the electric vehicle start-up could revalue to its historical P/S ratio of 1.31X—which implies a fair value in the neighborhood of $9.42/share.

NIO has upside revaluation potential if a number of conditions are met:

  • The EV start-up sustains its Onvo- and Firefly-related delivery momentum as consumers are shifting over to lower-cost EV brands
  • NIO continues to make vehicle margin gains and catches up to its EV competition, especially Li Auto
  • NIO lowers its operating losses and gradually transitions to an improving profitability profile
  • New EV product launches could help the company accelerate its delivery momentum: Firefly deliveries, for example, started in Europe last month and could be rapidly scaled given the market's taste for lower-priced EVs. With Firefly already having significant momentum, international expansion could be an accelerant for NIO's delivery growth.

NIO's Risks

NIO has material risks regarding its profitability trajectory, as the company has by far the highest operating losses in the industry group. The EV firm could therefore be forced to issue more stock and dilute shareholders as a result. I would be willing to adjust my opinion and my stock rating for the Chinese electric vehicle company if NIO were to see a contraction in its vehicle margins or failed to reduce its operating losses in the quarters ahead.

Closing Thoughts

NIO is seeing strong Onvo-driven electric vehicle delivery growth, and Firefly is off to a good start since it started to deliver low-cost compact EVs in April of 2025. NIO also submitted a strong outlook for deliveries in the third quarter, which implies that the EV start-up expects its current Onvo and Firefly momentum to continue, especially as European expansion picks up steam. While NIO missed revenue estimates on Tuesday, the EV firm had a decent quarter overall, defined by strong delivery growth and a small sequential improvement in vehicle margins. On the negative side, NIO still has to deal with high operating losses. I believe shares are still very attractively priced given the delivery backdrop, trading for less than 1.0X forward revenue, and I see a favorable risk profile for investors.
